 ## 🗺️ System Overview
 
-This codebase is a multi-surface Rust application that presents one shared runtime through several faces: a user CLI, an interactive TUI, app-server and exec-server processes, and supporting helper binaries. At startup, each executable first dispatches from raw argv, argv[0], and environment into the intended personality, then hardens the process, fixes global TLS/crypto behavior, and bootstraps the Tokio runtime. From there it discovers installation layout, CODEX_HOME, bundled helpers, shell and host context, and materializes any packaged executables needed later.
+This codebase is a multi-surface Rust application centered on a shared conversational/runtime core. One process image can present itself as the main `codex` CLI, an interactive TUI, app-server and exec-side services, helper utilities, or focused maintenance tools. At startup, binary dispatch interprets argv, argv[0], and environment to choose that surface, then early bootstrap hardens the process, fixes the global TLS provider, shapes PATH and re-exec behavior, and brings up the Tokio runtime.
 
-With the local environment known, startup assembles the effective configuration by merging managed, cloud, user, project, thread, and CLI layers into one resolved policy: features, models, plugins, MCP declarations, permissions, sandbox rules, and UI settings. Authentication then turns stored or environment-provided credentials into a concrete runtime identity, while the persistence layer opens and migrates the SQLite-backed state databases and recovers from corruption when possible. Once config, auth, and state are ready, the system constructs backend clients, refreshes remote catalogs such as cloud config and model lists, and brings up transports: stdio, Unix sockets, websockets, relays, proxies, MCP channels, and daemon-managed app-server listeners.
+From there, startup assembles the operating context the rest of the system depends on: it discovers installation layout and `CODEX_HOME`, materializes bundled helper binaries, probes shell and host environment, merges config layers and feature flags, compiles permission and sandbox policy, restores authentication, and opens the SQLite-backed state runtime with migrations and corruption recovery. Once config and identity are ready, the system builds backend clients, refreshes remote catalogs such as models, plugins, and cloud settings, and initializes transports for the app-server, daemon, exec-server, MCP, relays, sockets, and in-process clients.
 
-Frontend startup then chooses the user-facing path. The TUI connects to or creates a session, handles onboarding and terminal ownership, and enters its steady event loop; exec mode instead builds a single request and runs it to completion. In steady state, request dispatch routes UI events and JSON-RPC traffic into thread and session orchestration, which reconstructs or creates live conversation threads. Before each turn, prompt and context assembly freezes history, permissions, skills, memories, tools, and extension inputs into the exact model-visible prompt. Turn execution drives model streaming, compaction, tool calls, approvals, sandboxed side effects, and multi-agent/background workflows, then persists results and projects updated state back to clients and the TUI. Shutdown gates new work, drains in-flight handlers, closes agents, persists final metadata, and tears transports down cleanly.
+Frontend startup then turns those prepared services into either an interactive terminal session or a one-shot scripted request. After that handoff, the steady-state loop accepts UI events, JSON-RPC traffic, and internal messages, routes them to the correct thread/session, assembles prompt and context from history, memories, tools, skills, permissions, and integrations, and executes turns through model transports and streaming reducers. Tool calls, approvals, sandboxed commands, plugins, connectors, and MCP servers provide guarded side effects, while multi-agent workflows can spawn child threads and background jobs when a single turn is not enough. Results are persisted into rollout files and SQLite metadata, then projected back into app-server notifications, exec output, and TUI state.
 
-Across that main line, shared protocol schemas, transport/client libraries, observability and analytics, persistence abstractions, and utility crates provide the contracts, networking, telemetry, storage, and low-level support that keep all surfaces consistent.
+Shutdown reverses that path by gating new RPC work, draining handlers, persisting final thread and agent state, and coordinating daemon update/re-exec behavior. Supporting all of this are shared schema contracts, transport/client stacks, observability and analytics, persistence abstractions, utility libraries, and extensive test harnesses that exercise the whole lifecycle.

 # Execution backends and sandboxed command runtimes  `stage-14.2`
 
-This stage is the system’s execution substrate: it sits below tool handlers, RPC endpoints, and the TUI, and above the OS-specific process and sandbox primitives. Its job is to turn command-like requests—shell, unified-exec, code-mode actions, and apply_patch—into real running processes or controlled filesystem mutations, with streaming I/O, session tracking, approval checks, and sandbox enforcement.
+This stage is the system’s execution substrate: it sits below tool handlers, app-server RPC, and the TUI, and is responsible for turning “run this command” into a real local, remote, or sandboxed process with usable stdin/stdout semantics and policy enforcement. Its app-server and core orchestration layer accepts command/exec, process, shell, and unified-exec requests, applies approval and permission rules, and creates either one-shot runs or long-lived interactive sessions. Those sessions are then powered by the unified-exec backend layer, which provides PTY/pipe process objects, local and exec-server remote implementations, process managers, and cross-platform terminal plumbing.
 
-Execution-facing app-server and core orchestration receives command/process requests from clients and tools, converts them into executable plans, and manages interactive lifecycles. Unified-exec sessions and PTY/process backends provide the concrete process objects, PTY or pipe transport, local/remote adapters, and background-job management those plans run on. Patch application adapters specialize that path for model-emitted patches, validating syntax and policy before applying edits through the patch engine. Sandbox selection and Unix launchers decide whether and how Unix commands run under bubblewrap, Landlock, or shell-escalation wrappers. Exec-server filesystem sandbox services supply the matching local, remote, and helper-mediated filesystem backends used during execution. Windows sandbox provisioning and launch internals perform the equivalent setup and process creation on Windows. The directly assigned sleep tool is a lightweight command-like runtime that pauses execution while still integrating with turn history and interruption by new input.
+Patch requests flow through a parallel adapter path: they are parsed, vetted, and either converted into structured edits or executed through the same runtime framework, using the apply-patch engine to compute file changes safely. Sandbox selection and Unix launchers decide how commands are wrapped on Linux and macOS, including bubblewrap, Landlock, and shell-exec interception for escalation decisions. Exec-server filesystem sandbox services provide the file-access backends those executions rely on. On Windows, dedicated provisioning and launch internals create restricted identities, enforce ACL/network policy, and start sandboxed processes via legacy or elevated helpers. The sleep tool rounds out this stage with a command-like runtime primitive that pauses execution while remaining interruptible and visible in turn history.
